nativeRange.habitat: The Decorated Rabbitfish inhabits clear lagoons and seaward reefs with abundant coral cover, particularly areas dominated by Acropora corals, at depths between 1 and 30 metres.

diet.notes: Primarily feeds on macroalgae and filamentous algae; adults have been documented also consuming tunicates and sponges. Offer dried seaweed sheets (nori) via a lettuce clip, along with Gracilaria and caulerpa. Supplement with herbivore-formulated pellets. A well-fed specimen tends to be less likely to nip at corals.

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 3557x2668; tack-sharp hero shot on clean blue-grey background, whole fish, vividly shows the black snout-through-eye mask, honeycomb dark cluster above the eye, and fine blue wavy lines on a bright yellow body.
Rickard Zerpe; 4130x3098; sharp in-situ reef shot, whole fish, all diagnostic markings crisp and in full colour.
Bernard Dupont; 4416x2649; two individuals together (adults occur in pairs), in-situ over sand, clearly showing blue-spotted body and masked face.

summary: The Decorated Rabbitfish is a vividly patterned Indo-Pacific species with a yellow-streaked face and a blue-spotted body. It is generally considered peaceful and reef-safe when fed a varied herbivore diet. Like all rabbitfish, it carries venomous fin spines that require careful handling.
careNotes:
Wild specimens have been documented reaching up to 38 cm in total length, though aquarium-kept individuals tend to remain smaller, typically in the 20–28 cm range. Juveniles have been observed schooling around Acropora corals in the wild; adults typically occur in pairs.

The Decorated Rabbitfish is generally considered reef-safe when well fed, but has been reported to nip at LPS and soft corals (large-polyp stony and soft coral colonies) if undernourished — providing ample herbivore foods such as nori, Gracilaria, and caulerpa is strongly advised. Adults have also been documented consuming tunicates and sponges in addition to algae, suggesting occasional omnivorous tendencies, though plant material should form the core of the diet.

IMPORTANT: All dorsal and anal fin spines are venomous and can inflict a painful sting. Handle this fish only with a net, never with bare hands.

The species tends to adopt a mottled, camouflage-like coloration when stressed or sleeping. This is a normal response and not a sign of illness. Adults tend to do best kept singly in the home aquarium, though mated pairs have been maintained in larger systems. As with other members of the family Siganidae (rabbitfish), housing with conspecifics or other rabbitfish species is generally not advised unless the tank is exceptionally large. An aquarium temperature of 24–26°C is generally recommended for this species.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been reliably documented for this species. In the wild, adults tend to form pair bonds and are pelagic broadcast spawners. Replication of the necessary environmental cues (lunar, tidal) makes captive breeding essentially infeasible in home aquaria.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 38 cm TL wild, common 25 cm), temperature range (22–27°C), native distribution (Indo-West Pacific: Cocos-Keeling Islands and South China Sea to Gilbert Islands, north to Ryukyu Islands, south to southern Great Barrier Reef and New Caledonia), taxonomy (family Siganidae, order Acanthuriformes), and diet (algae, tunicates, sponges; trophic level 3.0). FishBase max size (38 cm) is notably larger than LiveAquaria's cited captive size (11 in / 27.9 cm); both figures recorded, with captive range (20–28 cm) used for adultSizeCm. Preferred habitat temperature modeled at 24.7–29°C on FishBase vs trade sources' 72–78°F (22–25.6°C); combined range 22–27°C used.

fish.breedingNotes: No reliable captive breeding has been documented for this species. In the wild, adults form pair bonds and reproduce as pelagic broadcast spawners (releasing eggs and sperm into open water), responding to lunar and tidal cues that are not feasible to replicate in a home aquarium.
